svg.title: Tight Coupling vs Loose Coupling with definitions
svg.desc: Full comparison diagram of tight and loose coupling in ASP.NET Core including definitions, code flow, and key traits

g.onclick: sendPrompt('Give me a simple real-world analogy for tight coupling')
g.text: A class directly creates and depends

g.onclick: sendPrompt('Give me a simple real-world analogy for loose coupling')
g.text: A class depends on an interface, not
a concrete class. DI injects it.

g.onclick: sendPrompt('What is the problem with new OrderService() inside a controller?')
sendPrompt('Why is a concrete OrderService hard to test?')

svg.text: Tightly glued — cannot swap or mock

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How does constructor injection work in ASP.NET Core?')
sendPrompt('Why do we define IOrderService as an interface?')
sendPrompt('How is OrderService registered in ASP.NET Core DI container?')
sendPrompt('Why is unit testing hard with tight coupling?')
g.text: Needs real DB — very hard to test

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How do you mock IOrderService in unit tests?')
sendPrompt('How many files change when swapping implementation in tight coupling?')
g.text: Edit every class that uses it

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How do you swap OrderService to a different implementation?')
sendPrompt('Explain the ripple effect in tightly coupled code')
sendPrompt('Why are changes isolated in loosely coupled code?')
g.text: Each layer isolated, safe to change

g.onclick: sendPrompt('Which SOLID principles does tight coupling violate?')
sendPrompt('How does loose coupling follow SOLID principles?')
sendPrompt('Summarise tight vs loose coupling in one sentence each')
g.text: Tight = classes glued together, hard to change anything.
Loose = classes talk through interfaces, easy to swap, test, maintain.
